steps.title: Support adult transmission
steps.content: The researchers predict that language-nest students will later transmit the Anishinaabe language. Since transmission happens in adulthood, the strongest support would show that the students keep the fluency and cultural pride gained at school. Evidence about later schooling matters because it connects childhood fluency to adult community teaching over time.

steps.title: Look for lasting effects
steps.content: A compares cultural pride but not language transmission. B shows benefits after transfer, not whether they last. C shows former students stay in the community at the same rate as others, without addressing fluency or pride. Persistence through later education directly supports the prediction. The answer is D.
